title: Anaheim, CA: A Case Study in Unleashing Market-Based Housing Abundance
author: Tobias Peter

Anaheim, CA: A Case Study in Unleashing Market-based Housing Abundance
Tobias Peter
Co-director and Senior Fellow, AEI Housing Center
Tobias.Peter@aei.org
Arthur Gailes
Manager of Housing Supply Initiatives, AEI Housing Center
Arthur.Gailes@aei.org
AEI.org/housing | June 2024

Summary
Anaheim officially adopted Mixed-Use Overlay Zones (Platinum Triangle) in 2004 with the intention of implementing the General Plan’s Mixed Use land use designation within the Platinum Triangle: a 1-square mile area (640 acres) roughly between Disneyland and Angel Stadium.*

Of the 640 acres, roughly half (320) is reasonably developable (the rest being composed of Angel Stadium).
As of 2023, about 40% (128 acres) of the developable overlay district had been redeveloped residential rental and owner-occupied homes.
About 2% of the overlay district has been developed per year.
About 4,500 homes have been built on 128 acres.
As built density (excluding streets): 44 units/acre.**
On a per sq. ft. basis, the assessed value of an overlay multifamily building in 2023 was 15 times the assessed value of a commercial property in the control group.
Anaheim averaged about 800 residential permits per year over this period.
Platinum Triangle averaged about 220 homes completed per year, or about 28% of Anaheim’s total housing construction.
Platinum Triangle’s ½ square mile developable portion comprises about 1% of Anaheim’s total land area.

* All acreage estimates are approximate.
** Assumes streets and sidewalks account for 20% of the redeveloped area
Source: AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.

A case study in unleashing market-based housing abundance
Anaheim officially adopted Mixed-Use Overlay Zones in 2004 with the intention of implementing the General Plan’s Mixed Use land use designation within the Platinum Triangle: a 1-square mile area roughly between Disneyland and Angel Stadium.
By doing so, Anaheim successfully implemented the housing success sequence and reaped the reward of naturally affordable housing.

By-right zoning
Enabled by-right multifamily development of at least 50 homes within the Mixed-Use Overlay Zone

Keep it short and simple (KISS)
Simple conditions for ministerial approval: building height, lot coverage, and unit size

Unleash a swarm of housing
Built 4,500 units in less than a square mile of previously industrial zoning

Source: Google and AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.

Natural housing abundance
Within the Mixed Use (MU) overlay zone:

More than 4,500 homes have been built since 2005, comprising roughly 20% of the developable land in the zone. Development is by-right and administrative up to the maximum density in the General Plan.
Because development is approved ministerial, it does not require a CEQA review.
The residential buildings range from 60 to 190 units per acre. There are no LIHTC units within the zone, but many are naturally affordable for moderate-income households. No price controls (“Inclusionary Zoning”) were required for development.
Starting in 2020, about 20% of the residences entered a workforce housing program that restricted them to households making up to 120% AMI.

Source: Google and AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.

Infill Development in the Mixed-Use Overlay Zone

Low value commercial/industrial properties were upzoned to high-value residences.
The apartments here start at $2,300 for a one bedroom, which HCD categorizes as affordable to moderate-income households (up to 120% of AMI).
No new commercial buildings have been built in the MU zone since 2005.
Despite only comprising 2% of the land area of Anaheim, the 4,500 homes built in this zone account for 26% of its housing built since 2000.
Given that the Angel Stadium comprises roughly half the zone’s area, the developable land is closer to 1% of the city’s area.
Fewer than 200 homes have been built within ¼ mile of the Zone, indicating a causal effect.

2006
2017
1781 S Campton Ave, Anaheim, CA
Source: 2022 ACS, HCD, Google, City of Anaheim, and AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.

Construction in Amenity-rich Areas

Despite being zoned primarily for industrial or residential use, dozens of amenities dot the periphery of the zone, including banks, restaurants, gyms, and a metro station.
Furthermore, it provides easy access to thousands nearby low-income jobs and a metro station.
Housing continues to be built in the zone; an 18-unit condo building was finished in 2022. And more ground continues to be broken.
Despite the relatively high number of homes built here, Anaheim could have built even more. The original developer plan for this 2022 condo was for 35 stories, as opposed to the three stories it became.
The non-residential zoning for this land remains industrial, another opportunity for areas like this would be to make it easier for mixed use buildings, rather than just residential, to be approved ministerially.

Jobs under $40k
Nearby Amenities*
* Each dot represents an amenity, and is colored by the type of amenity offered. This includes supermarkets, stores, playgrounds, post offices, etc. For the full key, use the Livable Urban Village map: https://heat.aeihousingcenter.org/toolkit/luv_map.
Source: https://www.midriseliving.com/anaheim-a-town-metro-1-0-what-could-have-been/, LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics (LODES, left map), SafeGraph (right map), AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.

Example of an unchanged commercial property in the control area
1645 S Sinclair St, Anaheim, CA 92806
Land use: Commercial property
Lot size: 164,000 sq. ft.
Assessed Value:

In 2003: $4.5 million (~ $27.5 per sq. ft.)
In 2023: $6.3 million (~ $38.4 per sq. ft.)

The assessed value increased 40% from 2003 to 2023.
Land use remains unchanged
(top: as seen in 2003, bottom: as seen today)
Source: Google, Attom, and AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.

Example of a converted property in the treatment area (Platinum Triangle)
915 E Katella Ave, Anaheim, CA 92805
Land use:

In 2011: Business Park with Medical Center, Design Company, etc.
In 2023: Multifamily building with 399 units on 5 stories.

Lot size: 287,000 sq. ft.
Assessed Value:

In 2003: Unknown
In 2023: $162 million (~ $564.8 per sq. ft.)

On a per sq. ft. basis, the assessed value of the multifamily building in 2023 is 15x the assessed value of the commercial property in the control group. In 2003, when both parcels were commercial properties, their assessed values were probably not too differently from each other.
Converted from a business park (top photo from 2011) to a multifamily building (bottom photo from 2023) in 2015
Source: Google, Attom, and AEI Housing Center, www.AEI.org/housing.
